metadata.title: Can We Change the Topic, Please? Assessing the Theoretical Construction of International Relations Scholarship
authors: C H R I S T O P H E R W H Y T E

abstract: A number of international relations scholars charge that the field suffers from academic religiosity: factional divisions along theoretical lines that limit research potential and create unnecessary bias against certain approaches. I explore this view of the field by applying a topic-modeling algorithm to a corpus of article abstracts from prominent journals in international relations. I generate a new dataset of information on international relations scholarship over more than two-and-a-half decades. I find evidence in support of claims that academic factionalism can negatively affect the research process. Paradigmatic foundations, though not present in most international relations studies, are closely linked to a large number of research topics and are associated with higher citation counts. At the same time, such works appear to be less likely than others to be "pathbreaking." My analysis also supports a narrative common in recent scholarship that citation counts, though somewhat effective in their measurement of scholarly impact, are sensitive to organizational features of the academic process.

sections.paper_id: Whyte - 2019 - Can We Change the Topic, Please Assessing the Theoretical Construction of International Relations S
paragraphs: A number of important works have examined shifts in the direction of the field of international relations. Some of this scholarship explicitly debates the "end of IR [international relations] theory" (see, inter alia, Lapid 1989;Holsti 1998;Dunne, Hansen, and Wight 2013;Jackson and Nexon 2013;Mearsheimer and Walt 2013;Williams 2013); many other works focus on how "interparadigm debates" negatively shape the field (for example, Laudan 1996;Jackson and Nexon 2009) and conclude that sectarian theoretical divisions, on balance, undermine efforts to generate greater knowledge about world politics. The argument, in short, is that such sectarianism breeds negative methodological and genealogical biases, but also encourages specialization and structured debate.

Proposed solutions involve, variously, a commitment to analytic eclecticism (for example, Sil and Katzenstein 2010), the reorganization of the field's intellectual landscape around "interests, interactions, and institutions" (Lake 2011), and an emphasis on causal mechanisms (for example, Bennett 2013). However, before we can weigh the specific merits of these suggestions, we first require more robust empirical data on both the nature and the scope of this "sectarian problem." Absent a better understanding of the topography of the field, it seems premature to settle on one or more alternatives.

I use topic-modeling techniques-previously applied successfully in the fields of economics (Kaplan and Vakili 2015) and computer science (Gerrish andBlei 2011, 2012;Kyung, Gill, and Casella 2011)-to analyze the generation and spread of core research topics in international relations literature. Topic-modeling techniques are a relatively recent development in the literature on machine-learning and Bayesian statistics. They aim to understand the distribution of themes and linkages among ideas across different documents. Topic-modeling tools provide the researcher the ability to describe text documents in terms of the word Christopher Whyte is a PhD candidate in political science at George Mason University's Schar School of Policy & Government. As of fall 2017, he is an assistant professor in the L. Douglas Wilder School of Policy & Government at Virginia Commonwealth University.
Author's note: Christopher Whyte is an assistant professor in the program on Homeland Security and Emergency Preparedness at Virginia Commonwealth University.

themes shared, over time, between them. I use abstracts of more than seven thousand research articles in eighteen prominent political science journals on international relations topics over a twenty-five-year period to construct such a model. I then test it against out-of-sample article-level and author-level data.

I use the method and resultant data to uncover the extent to which pathbreaking scholarship or paradigmatically informed priors characterize international relations scholarship. I employ the approach to answer a question at the heart of scholarship on the direction of international relations research: are we well equipped, in terms of our metrics and procedures, to observe and understand the interplay of factional interests and new scholarship going forward? Studies of the field, as well as assessors of scholar productivity, rely heavily on citation-driven metrics to communicate information about research impact. The topic-modeling approach allows for close-in validity testing of various assumptions implicit in the use of such metrics, such as h-index scores or impact factors. Here, I use the data to generate a measure of the innovativeness of research at the article level and, alongside different citation metrics, test against a variety of predictive factors.

My findings suggest empirical support for concerns about the role that paradigmatic training plays in influencing the theoretical construction of international relations research. On one hand, descriptive results show that paradigms do not dominate the field. This finding supports the Teaching Research and International Policy (TRIP) project scholars' conclusions (Maliniak et al. 2011), as do secondary results regarding the relationship between gender and citation practices (see Maliniak, Powers, and Walter 2013; also see Centra and Gaubatz 2000;Mitchell and Martin 2018). However, I find that paradigmatic foundations appear in a sizable minority of articles in the field. They are also linked to higher citation counts and occupy a pivotal position in the literature; they number among the topics most closely linked to the greatest number of subfields. Moreover, such foundations exist in works qualitatively indistinct from one another. This implies that the paradigms continue to play a role in the theoretical construction of research regardless of a turn towards midlevel theorizing (see, inter alia, Jackson and Nexon 2013;Lake 2013;Kristensen 2018) in the field. The inclusion of paradigmatic priors does not appear to make "pathbreaking" (such as topic-generating) outcomes of the research process likely. In short, the paradigms both play a distinct role in the theoretical construction of much research in the field and reduce the innovative potential of such work.

Secondly, I show that citation counts and related metrics do proxy for the "pathbreakingness" of scholarly output-at least to a degree. However, forces beyond simple scholarly valuation of research systematically play a role in shaping referencing behavior. The effects of institutional sponsorship and academic cohorts, in particular, seem to affect citation counts beyond the scope of what might otherwise match closely with innovative output. This finding fits generally with those of Colgan (2016), as well as past work that expresses concern over the way that scholarly training, cohort relationships, and the process of academic publication skew citation practices. Specifically, my results suggest that researchers must take steps to accommodate systematic biases when using citation-derived metrics to better understand the field, let alone when employing them to make decisions about such matters as hiring, promotion, tenure, or the stature of departments.

Finally, topic-modeling opens up broader possibilities when it comes to diagnosing the state of international relations scholarship. It provides researchers with flexible tools for identifying thematic trends in scholarship and in a much more granular way than other methods commonly employed in the field. In particular, it holds promise for assessing complex interactions between different communities of practice, such as the academy, policymakers, and pundits. I illustrate such potential applications in subsequent sections.

sections.paper_id: Whyte - 2019 - Can We Change the Topic, Please Assessing the Theoretical Construction of International Relations S
sections.header_raw: Are IR's "Sects" an Impediment to Progress?
sections.header_norm: are ir s sects an impediment to progress
paragraphs: Many international relations scholars view their fundamental task as producing better and more accurate knowledge about the world around us. But individuals and groups of researchers in the field often self-organize along factional lines, which may produce practices that harm the accumulation of knowledge. Scholars routinely argue that the interparadigm debate undermined the theoretical development of new research programs. They contend that self-described "realist," "critical," or other typeset scholars ultimately focus more on conceptual self-affirmation than on producing nuanced understandings of aspects of world politics (see, inter alia, Cohen 2007Cohen , 2008;;Blyth 2009;Maliniak and Tierney 2009;Lake 2011).

However, academic "sects" that form in response to strong support for a particular perspective on world politics do not necessarily harm the pursuit of knowledge. Different perspectives may, for example, bring greater understandings of the role of particular causal mechanisms. Contestation means forums within which scholarly consensus might be achieved and from which new efforts can be shaped. Moreover, different perspectives provide institutional and philosophical ways to organize and take stock of knowledge.

That said, Sil and Katzenstein (2010) argue that the intellectual benefits of factionalism come at a high price if not obtained within an environment supportive of methodological "eclecticism." Paradigms become the object of socialization in that they are taken as given or, conversely, judged as inappropriate for inclusion in studies in certain subfields. Likewise, scholars socialized in a particular context may view certain methods as inappropriate for use in social science research on specific topics or held up as the gold standard, despite clear shortcomings or demonstrable benefits.

The economic turn in the international political economy (IPE) field, for example, has diminished the prominence of qualitative analysis within top journal outlets (Lake 2006;Maliniak and Tierney 2009). This bias leads researchers to develop self-affirming research designs and to link the viability of particular methods to their personal or professional reputation (Lake 2011). When researchers exhibit these biases, they cloud the production of knowledge about world politics. Naturally, these tendencies and subsequent effects introduce some uncertainty to the field's effort to produce better knowledge about world politics.

If these biases are pervasive, do we actually know what we know (Jackson and Nexon 2009;Bennett 2013)? We can break these epistemological concerns into several empirically observable questions. First, to what extent are paradigmatic priors present in research? In other words, how often do scholars refer to major paradigms, and has this frequency changed over time? Secondly, how do scholars reference paradigms vary across topic areas and journal outlets? Lastly, do paradigmatic foundations lead to greater attention from peers? In answering these questions, we can capture the nuance of scholarship development in a way that previous studies have been unable. Recent works on the nature of the transatlantic divide in IPE (Cohen 2007(Cohen , 2008;;Blyth 2009;McNamara 2009;Weaver 2009;Weaver and Sharman 2013) and the TRIP project's set of surveys and other data (see, among others, Maliniak and Tierney 2009;Maliniak et al. 2011Maliniak et al. , 2018;;Maliniak, Powers, and Walter 2013;Hundley, Kenzer, and Peterson 2015;Byman and Kroenig 2016) have each outlined the intellectual tendencies of international relations scholarship but in both cases are sensitive to the limits of polling and qualitative research methods. This article provides a new, more granular typology of the field's core research topics and ideas to answer these questions.

sections.paper_id: Whyte - 2019 - Can We Change the Topic, Please Assessing the Theoretical Construction of International Relations S
sections.header_raw: Measuring the Topical Terrain of International Relations
sections.header_norm: measuring the topical terrain of international relations
paragraphs: Research in any discipline presents as an amalgam of a great number of philosophical and methodological inputs. In answering specific questions, scholars ground their responses in broader research traditions, as well as their own experiences. These habits produce unique patterns of conceptual and methodological linkages within a given field.

Understanding how to situate a particular piece of international relations scholarship relies on knowledge of the different parts employed in the piece's construction. Although researchers attempt to group scholarship under broad labels, the scholarship itself rarely-if ever-fits perfectly under any banner. Therefore, those studying the construction of international relations scholarship face a tension: broader labels-such as at the project or publication level-allow for parsimony but lose nuance, while more discrete measures miss important linkages. As some authors have noted (Bennett 2013; Sil and Katzenstein 2010, in particular), such labeling practices in research and teaching often promote misconceptions even about widely cited scholarship. Robert Keohane, for instance, is regularly referenced as a liberal scholar in stark contrast to Waltzian or Mearsheimerian researchers, despite Keohane's own acknowledgement and adaptation of "realist" assumptions.

Developing a greater systematic understanding of international relations research at the level of research topics and themes (as opposed to looking at specific publications, authors, or research outlets) is also desirable because new data can validate the metrics and procedures we use to observe, review, and reward valuable research. Citation-derived impact metrics-including h-index scores, journal impact factors, and simple reference counts-are produced through human effort and are naturally prone to potential skewness. Topic-modeling presents a method for systematically studying international relations topics and topical interactions that helps us verify widely used metrics like journal impact factors.

Given their prominence in assessments, citation counts, of course, have benefits. Any critique of using citationderived metrics must face the inevitable counterpoint that the "popularity" of scholarship is itself an important indicator of research potential. Not all new and innovative research is worthy of the same attention. An innovative approach may have significant limitations on its application. Likewise, new information on a substantive subject may fall short on generalizability. Thus, while innovative scholarship may be interesting to a subset of scholars, it may be of less value to the wider field. Citation counts are one mean of differentiating between an innovative, but niche, piece and one with broader influence.

But while citation counts might provide a reasonable mechanism for roughly differentiating between types of research, relying solely on citation counts as a proxy for influence overlooks the multitude of reasons a researcher has for referencing any particular piece of scholarship. As already mentioned, a piece might offer a methodological breakthrough, but present little substantive value to those scholars in the targeted subfield. Conversely, a piece may offer a critical substantive clarification that is only accessible to researchers in a limited area of focus. While each may end up with the same citation count, scholars relying solely on those figures will often miss the divergent underlying rationales thereof.

Moreover, there is significant evidence of various sources of alternative referencing behaviors. 1 Assessors might employ citation counts in a number of ways to proxy for productivity, but it is exceedingly difficult to control for the intervention of institutional constraints and social interconnections so as to understand the motivations for citation. Past literature on the international relations field, particularly in the American academy, points to several such factors as complicating agents for contemporary studies. The institutional goals of funding organizations like Carnegie Corporation, United States Institute for Peace, and others, for instance, directly influence the shape of proposal-and program-structuring by dictating the parameters of significant research. As Neff and Olden (2010) observe, institutional preferences can lead to artificially inflated referencing behavior, particularly in the case of 1 Much previous work has described the systematic manner in which certain determinants of peer reference pervade the academy, not just in political science. On authorship tendencies and institutional effects, see, among others, Morgan and Fitzgerald (1977); Durden and Perri (1995); Eisenhauer (1997); Kaplan and Vakili (2015). On gender in IR, see McDowell and Smith (1992); Hesli and Burell (1995); Maliniak, Powers, and Walter (2013). In other fields, see Grant and Ward (1991); Sonnert and Holton (1996); Long (1992); Symonds, Gemmell, Braisher, et al. (2006); Peñas and Willett (2006); Mitchell, Lange and Brus (2013).

self-citation (also, see Opthof 2013). After all, by buying into a particular research ecosystem, scholars might become path-dependently motivated to confirm the significance of certain pieces of work through citation. Path-dependent effects have been observed in other fields emerging from the structure of certain programmatic cohorts, a dynamic that speaks directly to concern of many scholars that factionalism pervades the international relations field and hampers our treatment of new approaches to research.

sections.paper_id: Whyte - 2019 - Can We Change the Topic, Please Assessing the Theoretical Construction of International Relations S
paragraphs: In this article, I turn to topic-modeling in order to study systematically themes across an entire field's literature. Probabilistic topic models allow for the "discovery" of main themes in an otherwise unstructured set of documents and are a promising way to see past the pitfalls inherent in the use of agent-assigned indicators (Huff 1990;Abrahamson and Hambrick 1997;Kaplan, Murray, and Henderson 2003). Scholars can apply topic models to a wide variety of data types to describe information structures without prior input by researchers (see Blei et al. 2004;Blei and Lafferty 2007;Ramage et al. 2009;Blei 2012). The discovery of themes or topics happens inductively and therefore is useful for researchers wishing to test existing assumptions about the nature of ideational, thematic, and associative linkages.

The simplest type of topic model, latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA), is a statistical model that assumes the presence of multiple topics in different documents (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003). Documents, whether they be journal articles, newspaper columns, blogs, etc. are viewed as an unspecified blend of themes and topics characterized by combinations of the different keywords, phrases, and term associations used by researchers. Distributions are obtainable because we work with the assumption that there is a fixed vocabulary generated by random assessment of the individual documents that make up the corpus.foot_1 This study applies LDA for a number of reasons, but foremost among these is that LDA improves the ability of researchers to map ideas in a given collection of documents. Specifically, LDA provides the clearest illustration of this article claim that topic-modeling can effectively capture the ideational topology of the international relations field. Alternative methods of approach are useful for research designs with particular requirements for mapping the functional utility of different topics or for extracting semantically coherent elements within a set of documents, but analytic needs should determine the researcher's model choice. Here, where the scope of the study is thematic variation rather than functional diversity across substantive topics, simple LDA makes significant sense.

sections.paper_id: Whyte - 2019 - Can We Change the Topic, Please Assessing the Theoretical Construction of International Relations S
paragraphs: In order to best assess claims about factionalism in the international relations field, I generate a topic model from a corpus of relevant journal article abstracts. I collect abstracts for a two-and-a-half-decade period and code for a variety of descriptive information. Abstracts are a useful unit of analysis when applying a topic model, as word limits require scholars to distill the important elements of scholarly context, method, and argument. Such tight parameters benefit the topic model and do not require the researcher to compensate by adjusting either the smoothing parameter, which might otherwise skew the representativeness of the topics "found." I use summarization software to generate abstracts for the small number of cases in which abstracts are unavailable (usually older editions of a few specific journals).

I select journals in two stages. First, I pull articles from each of the top twelve journals appearing in Garand and Giles' ( 2003) study of impact ratings in international relations. This approach expands on that taken by the TRIP project in that articles from every journal edition were collected. In using the LDA method described above, I use the corpus of journal abstracts to "find" cumulative topics prominent in the study of world politics. The method does not automatically label topics with certain significant keywords or descriptive terms; doing so would avoid the point of "discovering" thematic areas and describing them through reference to a particular mix of words found in the corpus. For demonstration purposes, Figure 1 above shows six topicsof thirty total topics found from a model fit to International Organization-with top key terms attributed to that thematic mix. Though the model provides no substantive labels for the excerpted topics, it is relatively easy to infer the topics' identities. Here, topic 00 clearly relates to scholarship on the development of the European Union and the politics surrounding European treaty processes, while topics 12, 27, 11, 06, and 28 refer to work on interstate conflict, sanctions, monetary policy and IOs, intrastate conflict, and the international system, respectively. The bag-of-words approach taken with the corpus of documents allows for the treatment of all text in the context of the broader collection. See Figure 2 for an example of how machine-coding of the text functions.

For the purposes of demonstration and testing for this project, I begin by applying a topic model to the collected corpus of documents representing international relations scholarship. Though the entire dataset includes the more than eleven thousand research documents that appeared in the eighteen journals across two-and-a-half decades, the first step taken is the removal of the approximately 3,800 articles not focused on world politics that appear in the field's interdisciplinary journals (APSR, AJPS, JoP, BJPS, and World Politics) over the period. Using the Stanford Topic Modeling Toolkit (STMT), which provides a documented and robust way to apply LDA, I then fit a topic model to the resultant dataset. The full list of topics "found" is available in Appendix C.

In using topic models, there is a great deal of potential variability that emerges from the arbitrary selection of model parameters. Researchers must make two choices and validate these choices in one of several ways. First, the researcher must determine the number of topics for the STMT program to "find." In this case, I specify that STMT should limit the model to seventy topics. I chose seventy topics based off peer validation across a series of model choices (variously choosing between thirty and 150 topics). I ask three experts separately to identify an appropriate threshold for topic integrity, based on several model iterations. I then repeat the validation procedure with an additional three experts asked to undertake the same task collaboratively. The trick involved in the validation procedure is one of balance. If the researcher requests too few topics, the algorithm might compress conceptually distinct, if linked, themes into one group, whereas if they request too many topics, the algorithm might produce groupings that are not semantically meaningful. Expert or other validation is necessary for a robust, usable body of topics. Second, the researcher must input two parameters to the LDA module when running the algorithm. One is a topic-smoothing parameter (α), and one is a term-smoothing parameter (β). In line with their work, I keep the α parameter at STMT's default. However, I alter the β parameter, which affects the concision of topic formation. During preparation, I use different β values of 0.1, 0.01 and 0.005 to categorize the different topic model results provided to experts and identify an appropriate level of granularity. A value of 0.01 provided the most distinct set of topics with fewer numbers of high-probability words assigned to each. Additionally, I specify an extensive stop list to exclude a broad range of functional words that might otherwise appear in a nonhierarchical topic model like LDA as unique topics.

During preparation, I use different β values of 0.1, 0.01 and 0.005 to categorize the different topic model results provided to experts and identify an appropriate level of granularity. A value of 0.01 provided the most distinct set of topics with fewer numbers of high-probability words assigned to each. Additionally, I specify an extensive stop list to exclude a broad range of functional words that might otherwise appear in a nonhierarchical topic model like LDA as unique topics.
sections.preview_head: In order to best assess claims about factionalism in the international relations field, I generate a topic model from a corpus of relevant journal article abstracts. I collect abstracts for a two-and-a-half-decade period and code for a variety of descriptive information. Abstracts are a useful unit of analysis when applying a topic model, as word limits require scholars to distill the important elements of scholarly context, method, and argument.

sections.header_raw: Paradigms and the Topology of the International Relations Field
sections.header_norm: paradigms and the topology of the international relations field
paragraphs: As long as the researcher employs appropriate validation techniques, applying a topic model to any large corpus produces a highly intricate picture of the thematic structure of the documents. In this first section, I consider the inclusion and interplay of the paradigms in the international relations literature from 1990 to 2015 and present initial data that describes how paradigmatic priors appear as thematic foundations. In the figures and tables below, I consider the proportionate volume of those topics found in the main seventy-topic model that (1) closely describe paradigmatic perspectives and (2) are difficult to characterize without reference to those perspectives. The topics containing paradigmatic foundations in this model are topic 02, topic 33, topic 34, topic 40, and topic 67, as seen in Table 1. I exclude topic 37-which features a mix of several paradigms and words common to describing the social science enterprise (knowledge, discipline, etc.) from consideration in order to focus on paradigmatic topics.

Chart 1 shows topic proportions for the sum of the paradigmatic topics outlined above. Split into five-year segments and delineated by percentage categories, the table suggests very little change in the distribution of paradigmatic work over time in the international relations field. Approximately seven of ten articles in each period contain no trace of paradigmatic themes (what I refer to as "foundations"), and an additional 12-15 percent of work only briefly refers to those topics (on average < 10 percent inclusion). Only a handful of articles (1-5 percent) present as solely (>50 percent inclusion) focused on the paradigms themselves. The remaining subset of articles (the remaining 12-15 percent) constitutes the bulk of research in which paradigms are centrally applied to different research topics. Initially, these results support the findings of Maliniak et al. (2011) in which TRIP data suggests a relatively diverse scholarly environment. Only three of ten articles topically link to paradigmatic priors in any measurable way and approximately fewer than 15 percent of all international relations literature demonstrates significant thematic engagement with the labels or core concepts of the "isms." In short, most articles simply do not reference the isms in a substantial fashion.

Articles that are more highly cited contain a greater proportion of reference to paradigmatic topics. Chart 1 demonstrates that the centrality (or lack thereof) of the paradigms has been consistent across the period of observation. Chart 2 presents longitudinal changes in the average article's paradigmatic content. The four graphs show the average proportion of paradigmatic topics included in articles every year, split by different categories. The top-left chart shows average proportions for all international relations articles from the eighteen journals surveyed across the period, while the top-right chart shows the same across only those articles that qualify as in the top 10 percent of all cited articles. The lower two charts show the same information averaged across only those articles that include paradigmatic topics, with the right-side graph selecting on those in that category in the top 10 percent of citations within the category. On average, paradigmatic topics account for a reasonably high percentage of topics included in all articles across the period.

Of particular interest in Chart 2 is the difference in referencing behavior between articles with high versus low paradigmatic inclusion. In the lower-right graph, the trend is mapped for only those articles that fall into the category of the 10 percent of top cited (annually) pieces of scholarship. Though these pieces in the lower-left graph exhibit the same relatively downward trend in terms of paradigmatic topic inclusion and the drop-off after the 1990s, highly cited counterparts exhibit no real change in concentration over the period. This finding suggests an enduring tendency to reward research couched in paradigmatic foundations and supports the conclusion that there has been little change in the concentration of paradigmatic foundations in scholarship over time.

Chart 3 shows that the degree of paradigmatic content varies depending on different subfields, as represented on a journal-by-journal basis. While trends are harder to discern here, the scope of different journals suggests a number of reasons why paradigmatic tendencies may be more "sticky" Kant, liberal, republican, human, social, intellectual, were, Doyle, hospitality, ethical, law, people, been, true, idealism, how Topic 40 classical, relations, history, thought, politics, was, essay, realism, theory, critical, realist, historical, Morgenthau, Thucydides, school, tradition, both, works, criticism, intellectual Topic 67 theory, powers, system, great, balance, relations, balancing, hegemonic, major, status, world, theories, structural, hegemony, relative, order, politics, realism, anarchy

Chart 1. Proportion of paradigmatic topics across articles by five-year period in some outlets than others. The sharp spikes in topic inclusion for the European Journal of International Relations, for instance, is unsurprising, given both the general scope of the journal as an international relations theory-focused outlet and the particular attention paid in several special editions to core issues of theorization in the field. In the security journals, it appears that articles in qualitative outlets such as International Security and Security Studies more consistently reference paradigmatic narratives or precepts. This conclusion seems stronger when we look at journals with historically more quantitative research, such as Journal of Conflict Resolution or Conflict Management and Peace Science, whose articles less frequently refer to the paradigms. We can also identify geographical divisions between journals based in the United States and those based in the United Kingdom. Though the information is somewhat limited, articles appearing in American journals more frequently include paradigmatic foundations than do articles in British outlets (including BJPS, IS, and RIS).

Chart 4, which details the percentage of articles that included multiple paradigmatic topics, supports the claim made in previous works (Maliniak et al. 2011) that paradigmatic innovation-for instance, cross-paradigmatic theorizing and conceptual eclecticism-is relatively limited in the international relations field. This result runs counter to the conventional wisdom that the field is becoming or should organically become more theoretically diverse (see, inter alia, Fearon and Wendt 2002;Moravscik 2003;Checkel 2012). With the exception of the years 1990 and 2001, most years in the past twenty-five have witnessed a relatively low number of efforts to meld core concepts in research. Though the modeled topics might initially seem to be an imprecise way of differentiating between competitive theory testing and fusions of competing perspectives, the fact is that the presence of individual topics without others signals a lack of crossfactional consideration. Those articles including one topic might certainly include competitive testing of competing paradigms, but no inclusion of significant thematic content from competing approaches suggests a lack of meaningful engagement "across the aisle," at least within the abstract statement.

Trends of topic inclusion for paradigmatic foundations are broken out further in Chart 5. Here, the identified topics are grouped according to the major research tradition they are most clearly associated with. Fortunately, there is little room for misstep here, as topic validation involved expert testimony wherein those participating were demonstrably able to differentiate between and within major theoretical traditions. Chart 5 tells us a story similar to that told in Charts 2 and 3 above; the early 1990s saw a rise in the inclusion of liberal and constructivist paradigmatic foundations, alongside a limited fall in the average proportionate inclusion of realist topics. Since the late 1990s, however, very little has changed in terms of how the different paradigms Chart 2. Average proportion of paradigmatic topics in articles per year by citation categories appear concentrated in the field. Liberal and constructivist foundations have arguably seen a minimal drop-off in popularity since the mid-2000s, while realist priors have risen with several banner years (2011 and 2014, in particular). The field as a whole, however, is not significantly less characterized by work containing paradigmatic foundations than it was twenty five years ago.

Finally, Chart 6 describes the production of paradigmatic and nonparadigmatic work by gender and then split out across the three associated major research traditions. There is remarkably little variation across both the paradigmatic/nonparadigmatic and tradition-specific graphs. Each graph shows the same trend; male authors produce roughly three-quarters of all international relations work (either solo or in single-sex groups), with the remainder is broken down roughly sixty-forty between female authors and mixed-gender groups of scholars. All-male author configurations represent almost 80 percent of all work with realist foundations-between 3 percent and 7 percent more than for other categories. All-female author configurations, at the same time, are responsible for more paradigmatic work that we might consider liberalism or constructivism. This apparent dynamic matches those observed in several past studies (notably Maliniak et al. 2011) that women are more likely to produce paradigmatic work outside of the realist tradition.

In the next section, I consider both evidence of the factionalism problem in the field and the validity of those metrics most commonly used in self-investigative studies of the field. In combination with the descriptive information pro-vided here, the testing below provides fodder for the idea that factionalism based on paradigmatic training and debate does have a negative effect on research produced in international relations.

sections.paper_id: Whyte - 2019 - Can We Change the Topic, Please Assessing the Theoretical Construction of International Relations S
sections.header_raw: The Thematic Construction of International Relations Research: Testing and Analysis
sections.header_norm: the thematic construction of international relations research testing and analysis
paragraphs: What role do the paradigms play in the theoretical construction of research? Do the most commonly used metrics of impact in use in the field appropriately capture information about new research? Scholars often cite factionalism as a problem for the field, and the interparadigm debates are the most commonly alleged culprit of sectarian skewness in research practices. However, the degree to which paradigmatic work drives other topical work and is thus reflective of the field is yet empirically unclear. Likewise, it is unclear if traditional citation-count metrics can effectively discern between which referencing behaviors are the generated by the ideational value of the research and which are driven by organizational (or institutional) conditions. Just what kind of "popularity" do impact metrics really report?
The Isms in Research and Practice

In addition to providing a great amount of descriptive information on thematic patterns in international relations research, the topic-modeling approach allows researchers to test whether paradigms drive research construction. Table 2 Chart 3. Average proportion of paradigmatic topics included in articles by year by journal below reports the top ten highest average Pearson's r scores (where original scores are translated to their absolute values) for topic correlations as a proxy for average value of the relationship between a topic and all other topics modeled for international relations. Comparing these scores is a method for reviewing changes to the thematic basis of research construction in the field. Higher average r scores for a topic indicates a greater number of linkages with other topics identified by the model. In other words, these topics coappear with more topics more frequently than other topics. Specifically, these higher scores can mean one of two things. First, higher average scores can mean greater reputational status across a broader number of articles than other topics. Second, a high average r statistic can also indicate a strong topical dichotomy between negative and positive associations (for instance, topics either strongly cooccur or rarely do). Either way, several topics related to the paradigms rank highly on the resultant list. These topics clearly relate to the realist and constructivist perspectives and are further linked to liberal perspectives only several spots below. This suggests that paradigmatic foundations are among the most pivotal thematic elements in international relations scholarship.

First, higher average scores can mean greater reputational status across a broader number of articles than other topics. Second, a high average r statistic can also indicate a strong topical dichotomy between negative and positive associations (for instance, topics either strongly cooccur or rarely do). Either way, several topics related to the paradigms rank highly on the resultant list. These topics clearly relate to the realist and constructivist perspectives and are further linked to liberal perspectives only several spots below. This suggests that paradigmatic foundations are among the most pivotal thematic elements in international relations scholarship.
sections.preview_head: What role do the paradigms play in the theoretical construction of research? Do the most commonly used metrics of impact in use in the field appropriately capture information about new research? Scholars often cite factionalism as a problem for the field, and the interparadigm debates are the most commonly alleged culprit of sectarian skewness in research practices.

sections.header_raw: Impact Metrics and Related Procedures: Are We Seeing What We
sections.header_norm: impact metrics and related procedures are we seeing what we
paragraphs: Think We're Seeing?

Here, I turn to the question of metric validity and test whether we really are seeing what we think we're seeing when we use impact metrics to "view" the landscape of scholarship in international relations. I do so via reference to a pathbreakingness metric, described below. The question here is a two-fold: is this an indicator of pathbreaking re-search, and are pathbreaking pieces of scholarship actually rewarded with peer reference? This is an important question for a number of reasons, not least of which is that a better understanding of factionalism requires a better understanding of how institutional elements of our field, such as how we coordinate and field research, interact with research's actual content.

To identify the emergence of new areas of topical research, I take the derived topics and set parameters for pinpointing when a particular piece is responsible for generating a new thematic area. In line with a number of recent applicants of topic-modeling techniques in social science research (see, inter alia, Kaplan and Vakili 2012;Mohr and Bogdanov 2013;Huang et al. 2014;Kaplan and Vakili 2015;Venugopalen and Rai 2015), this process is done via the identification of new topics into the sample of text and the subsequent selection of all individual documents weighted for new topic introduction after a period of one year. Specifically, new topic formation is identified using, roughly in line with past work, a 30 percent (0.3) threshold value for topic usage in articles that appear earliest in the period. Past work (Huang et al. 2014;Kaplan and Vakili 2015;Venugopalen and Rai 2015) has variously used a 15-35 percent threshold. The 0.3 value chosen for use here is the result of a secondary expert validation process, in which experts were asked to identify "first movers" from a chronological list of articles and abstracts. The 30 percent weight provides the closest match to these assessments and won out in robustness checks. Additionally, articles that are tagged above 0.5 (50 percent) for a given topic in the first four years are also captured as uniquely impactful. The year parameter is given to allow time for other scholars to absorb an article and its topic and then recirculate or reapply the topic in the form of new scholarship. On average, there are 1.52 Chart 4. Percent inclusion of multiple paradigmatic topics versus single-topic usage articles pegged to each new topic and 152 articles total that can be positively identified as responsible for the generation of new themes. A table of all topics reporting all common words recurring more than one hundred times can be found in the online supplementary materials in Appendix C, and the full list of articles tagged as innovative can be found in Appendix D.

Comparison of the overlap between articles appearing in the different categories provides basic evidence that some scholarship is "popular" for reasons beyond the inherent pathbreakingness or novelty of article substance. Figure 3 presents the discrepancy between those documents in the dataset that are topic-generating pieces of work and those that are merely well-cited. I present two measures of "high" citation-the top 5 percent of articles ordered by most citations and the top 10 percent of articles ordered by most citations.

Taken as a whole, there are 152 theme-generating pieces of work out of the approximately seven thousand documents included in the corpus. Of the articles that qualify in the top 5 percent of cited work in international relations in the corpus over this period, only fifty-eight also present as topicgenerating articles (only 16.6 percent of pieces in the category). In the 10 percent category, this number does increase in real terms (to seventy-nine), though it actually shrinks as a proportion (to 12.7 percent of work in the category). The result is fairly clear. There is a distinct discrepancy between certain pieces of work that present as topic-generating, influential scholarship and the subset of articles that are, by all regular standards of the functions of the academic community in the United States, popular. Moreover, this gap widens in the more constrained category of citations. There are two possible implications here. First, it is possible that there exists an effect that either limits reference to topic-generating work or disproportionately allows for boosted reference to work that is not topic-generating. Second, and just as noteworthy, it may be the case that the view of citation data as a representation of scholarly impact does an inadequate job of breaking down what "impact" actually means and that other factors invite reference.

Echoing research on the dynamics of scholarship in the literature on technology diffusion (Singh and Fleming 2010), the individual documents that constitute the corpus used in this study are coded for the presence of multiple authors. Previous research has shown coauthorship to be a solid indicator of greater productivity and innovation across various research fields (see, among others, De Maio and Kushner 1981;Wildavsky 1986;McDowell and Smith 1992;Endersby 1996;Fisher et al. 1998). Group projects tend to benefit from the input of multiple experts, often each with specific expertise in a particular subfield relevant to the cross-field efforts of a larger program. Efforts emerging from expert collaborations are often more timely, as multiple authors are able to work more efficiently with the suggested deadlines of a particular project. In this study, I use a simple dichotomous dummy variable to capture either single authorship or collaboration.

Simply testing for the effects of coauthorship, however, would be insufficient for controlling for possible sources of skewness in the gap between referential behavior regarding innovative and otherwise popular pieces of research. Coauthorship often comes in different formats and may arise from a host of different interpersonal or programmatic

Chart 5. Concentration of topics associated with major paradigms averaged by year relationships. To clarify, I do not suggest that there are major and systematic issues with bias inherent in the substantive pursuit of knowledge across all cases. However, both anecdotal evidence and some prior research (Price and Beaver 1966;De Maio and Kushner 1981) suggests that citation behaviors might change in the context of certain forms of sponsorship, with scholars feeling the need to legitimize past or contemporaneous research through reference. This might also present outside the context of institutional sponsorship (which is dichotomously coded on whether or not funding is reported in articles) as the result of entrenched interpersonal or professional relationships. The so-called "cohort effect" has elsewhere been identified as a potential deviating influence when it comes to citation, as scholars and emergent peers are driven by a range of motivations to reference within skewed parameters. A "core" research group forms and interacts with a "floating" population of scholars that cumulatively form an impactful cohort in a particular area of study (De Maio and Kushner 1981). I account for the role of both factors through dichotomous variables that register the patronage (or not) of a particular institution/program grant by reported expressions of support in the case of sponsorship bias and incidence of cohort publication measured by three or more publications by a group composed of at least 50 percent (when larger than three coauthors) of the authors in the case of potential cohort bias.

Beyond authorship, my coding regime took several descriptive factors into account. Drawing on previous studies of gender and citation effects in the field (particularly McDowell and Smith 1992; Hesli and Burell 1995; Maliniak, Powers, and Walter 2013), I construct a dummy variable that notes the gender mixture of articles' authorsessentially dichotomous as all male or not. I then test an expectation set by Maliniak, Powers, and Walter (2013) that gender citation gap in international relations exists but is significantly reduced in coauthored work. The next section also takes this analysis a step further by providing additional data regarding the relationship between gender, coauthorship, and innovation in the context of citational patterns. Further, I introduce several additional controls, including tenure status (all tenured or not), article age, and diversity of topics per article. Finally, I code for journal identification and, in line with the sections above, for paradigmatic focus. Paradigmatic focus is reflected in two dichotomous variables-a "foundations" variable, in which articles containing more than 15 percent of the topics identified above were coded 1, and a "labeled" variable, in which articles are coded on whether or not the text of the abstract specifically indicates usage of paradigmatic priors as springboard for the research involves. Including such an alternative measure makes some sense, as the "sticker price" of articles arguably determines research inclinations to read further at times.

Figure 4 shows three logit models-one that uses as the dependent variable the top 5 percent of cited articles (dichotomously coded), another that uses the top 5 percent average per year measure, and another that codes based on topic-generating articles. Again, the time frame under study is the two-and-a-half-decade period from 1990 to 2015. The break point of positive and negative impact on the appropriate dependent variable is 1.0, reported in the form of Chart 6. Breakdown of paradigmatic publishing by gender odds ratios. The results are robust across modeling choices, and the results of ordinary least squares testing using peryear average citation counts for articles are available in Appendix E. Here, the 5 percent and 10 percent thresholds are presented alongside the topic-generating dependent variable.

With regards to the tests of citation counts alone, the results show positive and significant values for a number of explanatory variables. Coauthor teams are more likely to produce work that attracts higher citation counts than are individual authors (one-and-a-half times more), as is work sponsored by a particular institution (about three Figure 4. Results of logit models

times more likely than work not sponsored) and research published by a cohort of regular collaborators (almost two times more than those not members of a cohort, as defined herein). These results show the clear value to be had in group effort-in citational terms in any case-and at least generally support the claim that there are significant benefits for research in coauthorship and the accommodation of institutional frameworks in defining research practices. Results are further borne out by similar, if somewhat reduced, findings for the second dependent variable, in which moderately different findings nevertheless support the same conclusion.

There are a number of possible interpretations of such results. Though there is certainly the possibility of skewness emerging from the impact of personal relationships and nepotistic practices, we might expect to see sponsored pieces of work or research authored within a cohort doing "better" for a number of reasons. For example, institutional sponsorship provides researchers additional means to conduct robust and meaningful research. Moreover, we might expect sponsored work to attract greater attention because of the piece's association with the sponsoring institution. Work sponsored by Freedom House or Harvard's Belfer Center, for instance, might receive more attention because those institutions both link to sponsored research and present themselves as "go-to" sources of information for a number of actors beyond just academics. Similarly, authors that have been sponsored in the past might reasonably be expected to benefit from their past association, with future work "boosted" by past visibility. This also speaks to expectations regarding cohort effects, as relatively small subfields might tend to encourage the formation of cohorts via reference to scholars' associations. Clearly, further investigation is required on the determinants of these findings, but one interpretation of the reduced finding for citations-per-year is that the greater attention shown to such work wanes over time.

Turning to author-level variables, the results show some support that a gender citation gap exists in international relations. Results show that there is a slightly positive relationship between gender-measured with two dummy variables for "all female" and "mixed gender," with male as the reference category-and citation. However, these results are only significant at 90 percent confidence and only for Model 1 (total citation counts). Tenure is not significantly related to citation metrics. However, other controls provide interesting insight into citation trends. Older articles are consistently more likely to receive more citations, as seen in Model 2. There are a number of possible interpretations of this, including rising volumes of articles being published in journals that are increasingly expanding or using online formats to deal with increased submission load. The relationship between paradigmatic work and citation counts remains relatively weak, though this might be further accounted for in future projects by controlling for the change in relative volume of such work over time.

From the current study, it appears that there are positive trends for paradigmatic pieces in that they are slightly more likely to be highly cited. Time-period controls might further allow for explication of the results regarding article type, which at present simply indicate that theoretical treatments are somewhat less likely to be cited than other types and that methodological expositions or introductions are significantly more likely to be cited than pieces of work in other veins.

There are a somewhat different set of apparent trends when it comes to testing for variation in pathbreaking research in IR, though it is important to note from that outset that the relationship between this metric and high citation counts is unambiguously clear. Even when a variety of factors are controlled for, there is a significant connection between work that leads to new topic areas in the field and work that is highly referenced by peer scholars. This finding supports the assumption that citation-derived metrics do proxy for pathbreakingness and productivity among scholars in the field.

However, a number of other conditions related to the process of scholarship production also drive the construction of impact metrics. In terms of the divergent trends illustrated in Table 2, neither coauthorship nor cohort effects seem to bear significantly on the odds that a particular piece of research will be thematically pathbreaking. One might expect that dedicated research teams attempt to deepen understanding of singular issues and do not aim to innovate on a broad range of topics in the study of world politics concurrently. Institutional sponsorship remains significantly correlated with innovation, though only at 90 percent confidence, showing that the benefits of financial support and institutional sponsorship manifest more as visibility than originality. Future research might consider that sponsored research benefits from institutional association over and above the intrinsic value of scholarship itself. At the author-level, the results support earlier work that attributes the gender citation gap in international relations to systematic bias only superficially. The lack of a significant result makes the case that gender and scholarship quality are not particularly related, despite the evidence provided from the use of citation-count metrics.

Finally, the evidence suggests that paradigmatic pieces of work are somewhat less likely to generate topics than are nonparadigmatic pieces of work. Pathbreaking material proves particularly likely to receive greater citation than either work below the threshold or work containing no trace of such topics. In essence, including paradigmatic foundations in research boosts the likelihood of reference by other scholars. For the topic-generating metric, however, we see a negative (though not significant) relationship between foundations and likelihood of producing pathbreaking work. Paradigmatically labeled works similarly appear somewhat more likely than others to become topcited articles in the field, but are similarly less likely to be topic-generating than others (slightly significant). This finding is not surprising for a number of possible reasons, including that paradigms are by their nature imbued with certain assumptions regarding core principles and thematic objectives. Nevertheless, it is interesting that paradigms both present broadly across the discipline as foundational for a solid chunk of international relations research and are associated in no way with pathbreakingness in new research. Future work might explore trends in the study of world politics by further breaking down this analysis across various periods of development in the American and other academies.
Discussion: What Does Topic-Modeling Tell Us About the Field?

In this article, I applied the novel and relatively unexplored (at least in this field) approach of topic-modeling large volumes of text to specific issues about the state of the international relations field's scholarship. Topic-modeling itself stands to help researchers not only better makes sense of the vast amounts of text available in the modern era, but also provides us better tools with which to critique our own profession. Here, the goal is to consider how both data and method bear on the goals of empirical mapping and metric validation. In particular, the goal is to achieve a far more granular analysis of these imperatives than has previously been possible and to lay the groundwork for future efforts.

Several conclusions are apparent. First, in distributional terms, the data shows that the field remains theoretically diverse and that reference to the paradigms-both directly and in the adoption of core concepts-is contained within a minority of world politics scholarship. Moreover, the information provided through topic-modeling of the eighteen-journal corpus suggests that, with only a handful of yearly exceptions, paradigmatic debate is the domain of only a small number of scholars. Fewer than 10 percent, on average, of all articles containing reference to the isms do not engage in interparadigmatic debate. In short, the paradigms do not obviously appear as conceptual foundations in most work undertaken in the international relations field.

Despite the apparently limited distribution of paradigmatic themes in much work in international relations, however, the paradigms do appear particularly influential in the thematic construction of research. Basic testing shows that paradigm-related topics are and continue to be strongly related to topical research in other veins over the period studied. Moreover, paradigmatic work is robustly linked to higher citation counts as measured by both total countper-article and average annual count. Reference to paradigmatic topics, in short, makes it more likely that a piece of scholarship will find referential success among the community of international relations researchers. Again, this result draws from the more granular understanding of thematic tendencies in the field allowed by topic-modeling and so reflects measurement of paradigmatic influence beyond the need to label entire articles as of the isms or not, an approach that may be subject to overgeneralization and coder bias.

Juxtaposed to this finding are the results in Model 8 of Figure 4 above that paradigmatic reference and labeling do not similarly indicate potential for pathbreakingness in research. Use of either measure suggests that such reference actually makes it less likely that research will be pathbreaking. Though this might seem unsurprising in that the paradigms are well-established schools of thoughtmaking innovation difficult-we would do well to consider the context of the data presented above that describes how the paradigms are distributed in the field. Though not the overwhelming majority that some might expect, paradigmatic references appear in a sizable minority of research over the period. Indeed, paradigmatic topics are among the most strongly linked to the broader topical body of work that constitutes international relations scholarship. Moreover, work referencing the paradigms appears only minimally different in content from the larger body of research that include no thematic trace of the paradigms. As is illustrated with the semisupervised topic model obtained and presented in Figure 3, there appear to be no major deviations in how topics are moving toward greater substantive treatments between the paradigmatic and nonparadigmatic categories.

Given this, it seems reasonable to interpret the pathbreakingness finding is in line with the concerns set out by Sil and Katzenstein (2010), Lake (2011), and many others. The paradigms are not referenced in most research work in the field, but they (1) are topically present in a sizable minority of articles in the field, (2) are linked to a highly diverse set of thematic areas of scholarship, (3) are linked to higher citation counts, and (4) exist in works qualitatively indistinct from others in terms of the trend toward greater empirical granularity in research in recent years. At the same time, the inclusion of paradigmatic priors appears to make pathbreaking (that is, topic-generating) outcomes of the research process unlikely or, arguably, less likely. In short, the paradigms both play a distinct role in the theoretical construction of much research in the field and reduce the innovative potential of such work. Naturally, the results presented in this article do not speak to the conditions of graduate-training as have some recent works (Colgan 2016, in particular), and so a full assessment of the mechanical link between paradigmatic priors at the author-level and research products is not possible here. But the findings do support the notion of a number of scholars that paradigmatic fault lines in the field do skew research outcomes in ways potentially damaging to the task of producing better knowledge about world politics. In this case, skewness presents quite simply as the suggestion, supported by the granular thematic data obtained from topic-modeling, that work referencing the paradigms reify certain topical traditions at the expense of innovative potential.

Third, the findings concerning commonly used metrics for scholarly impact provide an important clarification for scholars engaging in research on world politics, for professors involved in university processes, and for those professionals and editors at the reins of international relations'and political science's-various marshaling organizations. The results of testing show that citation counts and related metrics do proxy for the pathbreakingness of scholarly output to a degree. However, it is clear that forces beyond simple scholarly valuation of research substance systematically play a role in shaping patterns in referencing behavior. The effects of institutional sponsorship and academic cohorts particularly seem to affect citation counts beyond the scope of what might otherwise match closely with innovative output. This supports recent work regarding incentives to selfcite and otherwise inflate referential patterns for ulterior reasons (Neff and Olden 2010) and implies several important takeaways for academics. Foremost among these is the implication that citational information needs to be taken into account only insofar as assessments of scholarship can be qualified via secondary means. 4 For studies of the content of international relations scholarship and of the role of different theoretical or methodological approaches to research, this means the need to qualify assumptions drawn from simple descriptive tests using citation-derived metrics. If a degree of skewness exists in such metrics, then there is a significant need to unpack the determinants of the popularity of certain aspects of the field before drawing conclusions regarding the utility or common utilization of the same.

For decisions related to hiring, salaries, and promotion in the academy, it goes without saying that scholarly productivity should be squared against the ability of the utilized metric to proxy for that precise effect. University administrators should be aware of systematic biases present in referential metrics and should adapt processes to account for these biases, perhaps through the use of more telling index-building methods like the one applied in this article. In terms of the relative value of solo-author publications versus team efforts, of course, I do not mean to suggest that authors invested in a pipeline of coauthored research should not be considered as impactful as individual publication. Still, administrators and academics should be aware of the potential bias inherent in long-term cohort-or institution-based efforts highlighted here.

It is important to note these two implications-that factionalism clearly influences the theoretical construction of scholarship and that citation metrics reflect scholarly organization insofar as higher reference counts are predicted by more than just incidence of pathbreakingness-combine to support emerging work on the dynamics of research production in the IPE field. As noted above, Seabrooke and Young (2017) present network analysis results that support the notion that the IPE field can be explained by two logic of operation-first, where scholars organize themselves into communities that reflect broader intellectual debates with local characteristics and, second, where scholars select research design via understanding of competing, polar approaches to theory and methodology. Just as these authors find, the two-part results outlined above suggest that the same is broadly true of the international relations field beyond IPE: not only do paradigmatic foundations present broadly and predict artificially high citation counts, but the author-and institution-specific organizational characteristics of the field seem to explain the same.

Finally, the data presented above contain corroboratory evidence for the ongoing debate on the role of gender in citation and related practices in the international relations field. The results of testing support work done by Maliniak, Powers, and Walter (2013) and clarified in other research products (see, for instance, King et al. 2017;Tricco et al. 2017) that there does exist a degree of gender bias in citation practices in the field. Similar to the TRIPs effort, this study finds a small relationship (significant in the yearly model) between gender and citation, namely that female authors are less likely to be referenced than are male counterparts. Likewise, the data above shows that there is no apparent gender division in terms of the use of paradigmatic priors to construct research.
